topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise quiet afternoon. My thumb hovered over the 'Accept' button, then flickered back to the message preview. The name staring back at me was Marcus. Marcus. The name tasted like old chalk dust and sharp regret. My stomach clenched, a tight, unfamiliar knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the profile picture—a grainy shot from a summer camp, me looking painfully small and wide-eyed. A slow, almost involuntary shake ran through my shoulders. Why now? After all this time, after years of carefully building walls around that particular memory. I scrolled through my contacts, looking for a way to mute the entire application, but my fingers felt sluggish, as if moving through water. I finally tapped 'Ignore,' a gesture that felt woefully inadequate, and leaned back in my chair, staring at the blank wall opposite me, trying to reconcile the ghost of my past with this sudden, digital apparition.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the chime. It was an insistent, jarring sound that pulled me from a deep concentration on a complex coding problem. When I finally glanced at my phone, the sight of Marcus’s profile picture made the blood drain from my face. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I pressed my hand against my forehead, a sudden clamminess spreading across my skin. It felt like a physical jolt, a sudden misalignment in my entire sense of reality. *This isn't right.* I started tapping the screen repeatedly, a frantic, repetitive motion that served no purpose other than to keep my hands busy. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat—a small, choked noise—as I reread the request, my eyes darting from the username to the profile description, searching desperately for some hidden context, some explanation for this bizarre reawakening.

[story 3]

It was late, the blue light of the screen reflecting harshly off my tired eyes. The notification arrived without preamble, a stark red banner demanding attention. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. I didn't move immediately; I just sat there, frozen, the silence of my room amplifying the frantic drumming of my pulse in my ears. I slowly brought the phone closer, my gaze locking onto Marcus’s face. A strange, cold sensation washed over me, chilling me despite the warmth of the room. I let out a slow, ragged exhale, the air catching in my throat. I didn't know what to do with this intrusion, how to process this unexpected tether to someone from a lifetime ago. My hands rested flat on the desk, trembling slightly, unable to reach for the device, trapped by a sudden, profound stillness.
